UNICEF Child Welfare: A Global Perspective

Author

Kartik Katiyal

Published

April 13, 2026

Code 
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).

Introduction

This report explores two critical dimensions of child welfare using UNICEF data:

  • Child Deprivation — the percentage of children suffering five or more deprivations under homogeneous moderate standards.
  • Early Childhood Stimulation — the percentage of children aged 24–59 months whose caregivers engaged in 4+ stimulation activities in the past 3 days.

Together, these indicators reveal where children are most vulnerable and where development opportunities are being missed. We link these findings with macroeconomic indicators — GDP per capita and life expectancy — to surface structural patterns.

Code 
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd
import geodatasets
from plotnine import *
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
ind1 = pl.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_indicator_1.csv')
ind2 = pl.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_indicator_2.csv')
meta = pl.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_metadata.csv')

1. Global Child Deprivation Map

Which countries have the highest rates of children suffering five or more deprivations?

The choropleth below maps the average deprivation rate (Total sex, all years) per country. Darker shades indicate higher deprivation.

Code 
# Prepare: filter Total sex, average obs_value per country
dep_map = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col('sex') == 'Total')
    .filter(pl.col('obs_value').is_not_null())
    .group_by('alpha_3_code', 'country')
    .agg(pl.col('obs_value').mean().alias('avg_deprivation'))
)

world_countries = gpd.read_file(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datasets/geo-countries/master/data/countries.geojson"
)
world_countries = world_countries.rename(columns={'ISO3166-1-Alpha-3': 'alpha_3_code'})

dep_map_pd = dep_map.to_pandas()
merged = world_countries.merge(dep_map_pd, on='alpha_3_code', how='left')

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(14, 7))
merged.plot(
    column='avg_deprivation',
    ax=ax,
    legend=True,
    cmap='OrRd',
    missing_kwds={'color': 'lightgrey', 'label': 'No data'},
    legend_kwds={'label': 'Avg. Deprivation (%)', 'orientation': 'horizontal', 'fraction': 0.03, 'pad': 0.04}
)
ax.set_title('Global Child Deprivation Rate (5+ Deprivations)', fontsize=15, fontweight='bold', pad=12)
ax.axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Key insight: Sub-Saharan African nations consistently show the highest deprivation rates, with several countries exceeding 60%. South Asia follows closely. This geographic clustering reflects systemic poverty, limited healthcare access, and infrastructure gaps.

2. Top 20 Countries by Child Deprivation

Which specific countries rank highest, and does gender matter?

Code 
# Top 20 countries by avg deprivation (Total), then show Male vs Female split
top20_countries = (
    ind1
    .filter((pl.col('sex') == 'Total') & pl.col('obs_value').is_not_null())
    .group_by('country')
    .agg(pl.col('obs_value').mean().alias('avg_deprivation'))
    .sort('avg_deprivation', descending=True)
    .head(20)
    .select('country')
)

top20_gendered = (
    ind1
    .filter(
        pl.col('sex').is_in(['Male', 'Female']) &
        pl.col('obs_value').is_not_null() &
        pl.col('country').is_in(top20_countries['country'])
    )
    .group_by('country', 'sex')
    .agg(pl.col('obs_value').mean().alias('avg_deprivation'))
    .sort('avg_deprivation', descending=True)
)

df_plot = top20_gendered.to_pandas()

(
    ggplot(df_plot, aes(x='reorder(country, avg_deprivation)', y='avg_deprivation', fill='sex'))
    + geom_bar(stat='identity', position='dodge', width=0.7)
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_manual(values={'Male': '#2196F3', 'Female': '#E91E63'})
    + labs(
        title='Top 20 Countries: Child Deprivation by Gender',
        x='Country',
        y='Average Deprivation Rate (%)',
        fill='Sex'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10, 7),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face='bold'),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=8)
    )
)

Key insight: Gender disparities are relatively small within the same country — deprivation is largely structural rather than sex-specific. Niger, Chad, and Guinea consistently top the list, with deprivation rates above 70%.

3. Early Childhood Stimulation: Regional Breakdown

How does early stimulation coverage vary across regions?

Code 
africa_codes = ['DZ','AO','BJ','BW','BF','BI','CM','CV','CF','TD','KM','CG','CD','CI','DJ','EG','GQ','ER','ET','GA','GM','GH','GN','GW','KE','LS','LR','LY','MG','MW','ML','MR','MU','MA','MZ','NA','NE','NG','RW','ST','SN','SL','SO','ZA','SS','SD','SZ','TZ','TG','TN','UG','ZM','ZW']
asia_codes = ['AF','AM','AZ','BH','BD','BT','BN','KH','CN','CY','GE','IN','ID','IR','IQ','IL','JP','JO','KZ','KW','KG','LA','LB','MO','MY','MV','MN','MM','NP','KP','OM','PK','PS','PH','QA','SA','SG','LK','SY','TW','TJ','TH','TL','TR','TM','AE','UZ','VN','YE']
americas_codes = ['AG','AR','BS','BB','BZ','BO','BR','CA','CL','CO','CR','CU','DM','DO','EC','SV','GD','GT','GY','HT','HN','JM','MX','NI','PA','PY','PE','KN','LC','VC','SR','TT','US','UY','VE']
europe_codes = ['AL','AD','AT','BY','BE','BA','BG','HR','CZ','DK','EE','FI','FR','DE','GR','HU','IS','IE','IT','XK','LV','LI','LT','LU','MT','MD','MC','ME','NL','MK','NO','PL','PT','RO','RU','SM','RS','SK','SI','ES','SE','CH','UA','GB','VA']

def assign_continent(code):
    if code in africa_codes: return 'Africa'
    if code in asia_codes: return 'Asia'
    if code in americas_codes: return 'Americas'
    if code in europe_codes: return 'Europe'
    return 'Oceania/Other'

stim_total = (
    ind2
    .filter((pl.col('sex') == 'Total') & pl.col('obs_value').is_not_null())
    .with_columns(
        pl.col('alpha_2_code').map_elements(assign_continent, return_dtype=pl.Utf8).alias('continent')
    )
    .group_by('country', 'alpha_2_code', 'continent')
    .agg(pl.col('obs_value').mean().alias('avg_stimulation'))
)

continent_avg = (
    stim_total
    .group_by('continent')
    .agg(pl.col('avg_stimulation').mean().alias('avg_stimulation'))
    .sort('avg_stimulation', descending=True)
)

(
    ggplot(continent_avg.to_pandas(), aes(x='reorder(continent, avg_stimulation)', y='avg_stimulation', fill='continent'))
    + geom_bar(stat='identity', width=0.6)
    + geom_text(aes(label='avg_stimulation.round(1).astype(str) + "%"'), nudge_y=1, size=10)
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_brewer(type='qual', palette='Set2')
    + labs(
        title='Average Early Childhood Stimulation Rate by Region',
        x='Region',
        y='Average Stimulation Rate (%)',
        fill='Region'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(9, 5), plot_title=element_text(size=13, face='bold'), legend_position='none')
)

Key insight: Europe and the Americas show higher stimulation rates, reflecting stronger social safety nets and parental awareness programs. Africa and parts of Asia show lower engagement — areas that would benefit most from targeted early childhood programs.

4. Scatter Plot: GDP per Capita vs. Child Deprivation

Do wealthier countries have lower child deprivation rates?

Code 
dep_latest = (
    ind1
    .filter((pl.col('sex') == 'Total') & pl.col('obs_value').is_not_null())
    .sort('time_period', descending=True)
    .group_by('country', 'alpha_3_code')
    .agg([
        pl.col('obs_value').first().alias('deprivation'),
        pl.col('time_period').first().alias('year')
    ])
)

gdp_latest = (
    meta
    .filter(pl.col('GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)').is_not_null())
    .sort('year', descending=True)
    .group_by('alpha_3_code')
    .agg([
        pl.col('GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)').first().alias('gdp_pc'),
        pl.col('Life expectancy at birth, total (years)').first().alias('life_exp')
    ])
)

scatter_df = dep_latest.join(gdp_latest, on='alpha_3_code', how='inner').to_pandas()
scatter_df = scatter_df.dropna(subset=['gdp_pc', 'deprivation'])

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x='gdp_pc', y='deprivation'))
    + geom_point(aes(color='life_exp'), size=3, alpha=0.8)
    + geom_smooth(method='lm', color='#E53935', se=True, linetype='dashed')
    + scale_x_log10()
    + scale_color_gradient(low='#FF7043', high='#1565C0', name='Life Expectancy\n(years)')
    + labs(
        title='GDP per Capita vs. Child Deprivation Rate',
        subtitle='Log scale on X axis | Colour = Life Expectancy',
        x='GDP per Capita (USD, log scale)',
        y='Child Deprivation Rate (%)'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 6), plot_title=element_text(size=13, face='bold'))
)

Key insight: There is a clear negative relationship — as GDP per capita increases, child deprivation rates fall sharply. Countries with low life expectancy (orange dots) cluster in the high-deprivation, low-GDP quadrant, confirming that poverty, poor health, and child deprivation are tightly interconnected.

5. Life Expectancy Trends Over Time by Region

How has life expectancy evolved across regions since 1990?

Code 
life_trend = (
    meta
    .filter(pl.col('Life expectancy at birth, total (years)').is_not_null())
    .filter(pl.col('year') >= 1990)
    .with_columns(
        pl.col('alpha_2_code').map_elements(assign_continent, return_dtype=pl.Utf8).alias('continent')
    )
    .group_by('continent', 'year')
    .agg(pl.col('Life expectancy at birth, total (years)').mean().alias('avg_life_exp'))
    .sort('year')
)

(
    ggplot(life_trend.to_pandas(), aes(x='year', y='avg_life_exp', color='continent', group='continent'))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=1.5, alpha=0.6)
    + scale_color_brewer(type='qual', palette='Dark2')
    + labs(
        title='Life Expectancy Trends by Region (1990–Present)',
        x='Year',
        y='Average Life Expectancy (years)',
        color='Region'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 6), plot_title=element_text(size=13, face='bold'))
)

Key insight: Life expectancy has risen globally across all regions since 1990. Africa, which started lowest, has shown the steepest gains — reflecting improvements in healthcare access, HIV/AIDS treatment, and child mortality reduction. Europe and the Americas remain consistently highest.

Conclusion

This analysis reveals four interconnected truths:

  1. Child deprivation is geographically concentrated — Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia bear a disproportionate burden, driven by structural poverty.
  2. Gender is not the primary driver — Deprivation is roughly equal across boys and girls within the same country; the structural environment matters more.
  3. Wealth correlates strongly with child welfare — GDP per capita is a reliable predictor of both deprivation rates and early stimulation engagement.
  4. Progress is happening but unevenly — Life expectancy is rising globally, but the gap between regions remains wide. The countries with the greatest needs are also the ones where early childhood investment is lowest.

Targeted interventions — particularly in early stimulation and poverty reduction — can break this cycle. The data makes the case clearly: where children thrive, nations thrive.
